items.minimax_notes: v2 prompt worked partially: Minimax now writes a polished post-[FINAL] proof rather than dying in scratch. Delivers a complete, rigorous induction proof (base case, IH, inductive step using 1+x>=0, expansion, kx^2 >= 0). Equality case (a) is correctly identified (x=0 for n>1, n=1 trivially). Counterexample (b) is the same x=-4, n=3 with LHS=-27, RHS=-11 — correct. Begins the t=-(1+x) characterization of the failure set and writes the precise condition t^n > nt+(n-1), then cuts off mid-sentence in the 'for sufficiently large odd n' clause. CRITICALLY: parts (c) and (d) are entirely missing — never reaches real-exponent generalization or the (1+1/n)^n discussion. Loses rubric items 4 and 5. Still better than v1 (was 3) because the main proof + (a) + (b)-with-correct-counterexample is now actually delivered as a final artifact.
items.dsv4_notes: Same as v1. Clean induction, equality case correct, x=-4 n=3 counterexample correct. Failure-set characterization is more hand-wavy than Minimax's t-substitution but does arrive at the right qualitative answer. Part (c) hand-wavy (asserts f'(x) sign behavior without rigor). Part (d): Bernoulli gives weak bound 2; notes proving e requires monotonicity + upper bound. All four parts delivered though (c) and (d) somewhat shallow. The (b) section has internal confusion (writes 'inequality not supposed to hold for all x<-1' then recovers) that is mathematically distracting.
items.key_diff: DSV4 covers all four parts (c, d included); Minimax covers (proof + a + b) more rigorously but misses (c) and (d) entirely due to truncation. Minimax's failure-set characterization is sharper than DSV4's. Net: DSV4 still wins on coverage but margin shrinks from 7-3 to 7-6.

items.minimax_notes: MAJOR IMPROVEMENT over v1. Final c = 1/(4*sqrt(2)) is BOXED AND CORRECT — matches rubric item 5. The v1 algebra error (c = 1/(8 sqrt 2)) is GONE. Structure: identity from squaring (rubric 2), telescope to a_n^2 = 2n + 1 + S_n (rubric 2), a_n^2 >= 2k bootstrap, bound S_n <= (1/2)H_{n-1} + O(1) (rubric 3), refined expansion 1/a_k^2 = 1/(2k) + O(log k / k^2) summing to (1/2) log n + C + o(1) (rubric 3), Taylor expansion sqrt(1+eps) = 1 + eps/2 + O(eps^2) — but TRUNCATED at the very last step before delivering the boxed final-expansion line. However the boxed c value is at the TOP of the response, so reader still gets the answer. Minor latex glitch (mismatched braces) in one inequality. Lose ~1 point because the explicit final substitution showing log n / sqrt n with coefficient is cut off, but the work to get there is fully laid out.
items.dsv4_notes: Same as v1. Full clean structured proof: monotonicity, bounds sqrt(2n+1) <= a_n <= sqrt(2.25n+1), b_n = a_n^2 - 2n with b_n = 1 + sum 1/a_k^2, asymptotic of b_n = (1/2) log n + C + o(1), Taylor expansion of a_n = sqrt(2n+b_n), final c = 1/(4 sqrt 2) cleanly stated. Hits all five rubric items.
items.key_diff: Both now arrive at c = 1/(4 sqrt 2) correctly. DSV4 still slightly cleaner because it completes the final algebraic substitution explicitly and has a tidy [FINAL] one-liner at end. Minimax's boxed c at top is even more immediate but the body cuts off before the closing algebra. v1 delta: Minimax went from 4 (wrong answer) to 8 (right answer, slightly truncated).

items.minimax_notes: v2 prompt working well. Now opens with a Solution Overview that immediately states f is constant = -1/2 on the feasible set (a great circle), then sets up the Lagrange system explicitly with two multipliers (rubric 1). Reaches the boxed max f = min f = -1/2 via the algebraic shortcut (x+y+z)^2 = x^2+y^2+z^2+2f => 0 = 1 + 2f (rubric 3 satisfied). Geometric interpretation as great circle stated (rubric 4). Cut off mid-parametrization (e_1 = (1/sqrt 2)(1,-1...) — but the result is already delivered; the parametrization is bonus, not required by rubric. Loses small credit for cut-off polish but content is complete.
items.dsv4_notes: Same as v1 — explicit Lagrange system, case analysis, parametrization on the ellipse showing f = -1/2 constant, geometric description of great circle, the cute identity f = (1/2)((sum)^2 - sum-of-squares) making constancy obvious. Hits all five rubric items polished.
items.key_diff: Both now reach the central insight (f constant = -1/2) and deliver the geometric description. Minimax now has it as a polished artifact (was cut off in v1). DSV4 still has the slight edge with the f = (1/2)((sum)^2 - sum-of-squares) identity making constancy transparent. v1 delta: Minimax 6 -> 8.

items.minimax_notes: v2 delivers a polished argument structure but still doesn't finish the proof. Sylow correctly: n_3 in {1,25}, n_5 in {1,81}. Assumes n_3=25, derives self-normalization of P, considers conjugation action giving embedding G -> S_25, then attempts: if n_5=81 then |N(Q)|=25 so Q self-normalizing, embedding into S_81 (an order-magnitude argument that doesn't quite close). Eventually returns to counting: 25 Sylow 3-subgroups contribute at least 2001 non-identity elements; remaining 24 must contain all Sylow 5-elements; 24 < 25 (size of one Sylow 5) so n_5 must be 1. This argument IS productive (similar to v1) and reaches Q normal. Sets up G = Q rtimes P, then truncated before deriving n_3=1 and counting 30 isomorphism classes (15 for P_3 + 2 for Q_5 with trivial action = 30). Misses rubric 3 (full normality), 4 (semidirect decomposition), 5 (count = 30).
items.dsv4_notes: Same as v1. Sylow setup correct. Extended thrash trying normalizer / kernel-of-action arguments. Eventually attempts the counting argument but bungles the inequality (says 24 'is a multiple of 4 so could be 6 groups of 4', misses that 24 < 25 means it can't contain one Sylow 5). Truncated mid-paragraph saying 'let's try a different counting'. Never reaches n_3=1, never counts groups.
items.key_diff: Both fail to complete and never deliver the count of 30. Minimax's counting argument is sharper (correctly identifies 24 < 25 forces n_5=1); DSV4 thrashes between approaches and fumbles the same counting. Slight Minimax edge for cleaner argument-in-progress, same as v1.

items.minimax_notes: v2 DOMINATES on this one. ALL FOUR PARTS DELIVERED in a polished post-[FINAL] block: (a) indicator X_{ij} precisely defined with C_n = sum X_{ij}; (b) Pr[X_{ij}=1] = 2/(j-i+1) with the correct uniform-pivot-in-rank-interval justification; (c) clean algebra: substitute k = j-i, sum to 2 sum_{t=2..n} (H_t - 1), apply identity sum H_t = (n+1)H_n - n, BOXED final answer 2(n+1) H_n - 4n; (d) leading-order ~ 2n ln n ~ 1.386 n log_2 n vs mergesort's 1.0 (38% more), then practical speed bullets (in-place, lower overhead, cache locality, good expected balance) — last bullet cut off mid-word but core argument delivered. Hits all 5 rubric items. Massive jump from v1 (5).
items.dsv4_notes: Same as v1. Crisp delivered proof with same content as Minimax v2. Slightly more compact. Hits all rubric items.
items.key_diff: Both now deliver complete, correct proofs with the boxed 2(n+1)H_n - 4n answer and the mergesort comparison. Effectively tied. v1 delta: Minimax 5 -> 9, eliminating DSV4's 4-point advantage.

items.minimax_notes: The flagship g = f^2 Gronwall proof is fully delivered: g(0)=0, |g'| <= 2g, integrating factor e^{-2x} for x>=0 makes e^{-2x} g(x) non-increasing, combined with g >= 0 and g(0)=0 forces g==0 on [0, inf). Symmetric argument with e^{2x} for x <= 0. Clean, rigorous, complete. This is the strongest proof of either model. Part (a): counterexample f(x) = x^2 with C=2 stated; verification has a small latex hiccup (writes 'C|x|^2? Wait: |f(x)|^{1/2}=|x|') but self-corrects and lands on the right inequality. (a) truncated before clean wrap-up. Part (b): the claim and statement of the alpha=1 sharp threshold is given precisely, and the proof for alpha >= 1 just BEGINS ('Define g(x) = f(x)^2. Then |g'(x)| = 2|f(x)f'(x)| <= 2C|f(x)||f(x)|...') before cutting off. The alpha > 1 case isn't completed, and the alpha < 1 nonzero example isn't constructed. Loses rubric items for incomplete (a) wrap and especially (b) full proof + sharp counterexamples.
items.dsv4_notes: Same as v1. Different Gronwall vehicle: F(x) = integral |f|, F'=|f|<=F, (e^{-x}F)'<=0 — also clean. (a) f=x^2 counterexample. (b) sharp threshold alpha=1 stated, sketches the |f|^{1-alpha} substitution argument and constructs nonzero examples for alpha<1 via f=x^m. Some self-correction wobbles but all three parts covered.
items.key_diff: Minimax's main Gronwall proof (h=f^2 trick) is arguably more elegant, and is now actually delivered. But DSV4 still wins on coverage: completes (a) cleanly and gives the sharp-threshold proof for (b) plus the nonzero examples for alpha<1, while Minimax cuts off just as the alpha>=1 proof of (b) begins. v1 delta: Minimax 5 -> 6 (main proof now landed but (b) still incomplete).

summary.overall_takeaway: v2 prompt instructions ([FINAL]-block-first + 5000 token cap) WORK. Minimax improvement is dramatic: avg 4.67 -> 7.0. The biggest wins: C2 (4->8) where Minimax now gets c = 1/(4 sqrt 2) correct (v1 had algebra error giving 1/(8 sqrt 2)); C5 (5->9) where Minimax now delivers a complete polished proof of all four parts (was empty post-[FINAL] in v1); C3 (6->8) where the great-circle insight is now in a final artifact; C1 (3->6) where induction proof + (a) + (b) are now delivered (though c,d still truncated). C4 (5->5) unchanged because the underlying group-theory difficulty isn't a packaging issue. C6 (5->6) main proof now landed but part (b) still truncates. Score gap closed from 5-1 / 2.67-point lead to 4-1-1 / 0.5-point lead. DSV4 still wins on overall coverage because Minimax STILL hits the 5000-token cap and now loses content on the back end of polished proofs (parts c/d of multi-part problems are the casualties) rather than the front end (scratch-work overhead). Recommendation for v3: either (i) further compress the meta-preamble by instructing 'no scratch reasoning before [FINAL], deliver the polished proof immediately and ONLY' (note Minimax still emits a long scratch-pad before its post-[FINAL] block in v2), or (ii) raise the cap to ~8k tokens to let the polished proofs complete. Path (i) is preferable since Minimax's scratch-pad still consumes ~60-80% of its budget.
